title: California Dog Bite Lawyer | Frank Penney Injury Law
metaDescription: California dog bite lawyer for victims of dog attacks. Strict liability law is on your side. $1B+ recovered. Free consultation 24/7. No fee unless we win.
heroSubheadline: Bitten by a Dog? Strict Liability Laws Are on Your Side.
whyText: California's dog bite statute imposes strict liability on owners — meaning you don't have to prove the owner did anything wrong. Frank Penney Injury Law has 30+ years of experience securing fair compensation for dog attack victims across California.
citiesTitle: California Dog Bite Lawyers Across Northern California
citiesLede: Frank Penney Injury Law represents dog bite victims across Sacramento, Roseville, Fairfield, Stockton, Modesto, Chico, and the rest of Northern California. Under California Civil Code §3342, dog owners are strictly liable for bites on public property or when the victim was lawfully on private property — meaning the owner is responsible whether or not the dog had a history of biting. We handle every type of dog attack case: family-pet attacks, neighbor-dog incidents, off-leash bites at parks, and attacks by aggressive breeds.
serviceDescription: Representing California dog bite and dog attack victims with $1 Billion+ recovered. We handle strict-liability dog bite cases throughout Sacramento, Roseville, Fairfield, Stockton, Modesto, and Chico.

faq.q: Is California a 'strict liability' state for dog bites?
faq.a: Yes. Under California Civil Code §3342, dog owners are strictly liable for bites in public places or when the victim was lawfully on private property. The 'one bite rule' (where owners are only liable if they knew the dog was dangerous) does not apply — the owner is responsible whether or not the dog had any prior history.

faq.q: Who pays my medical bills after a dog bite?
faq.a: Most homeowners' and renters' insurance policies cover dog bites, often with coverage of $100,000 to $500,000 or more. The dog owner's policy is usually the primary source of recovery. We handle all communication with the insurer so you can focus on healing.

faq.q: What if the dog has never bitten anyone before?
faq.a: Doesn't matter. California's strict-liability statute applies regardless of the dog's prior history. Even a dog with no aggressive past creates owner liability if it bites someone in a public place or someone lawfully on private property.

faq.q: What if I was bitten while trespassing?
faq.a: Strict liability under §3342 applies only when the victim was lawfully on private property — so trespassers may not be covered by the strict liability statute. However, victims who were invited or had a lawful purpose (mail carrier, delivery driver, social guest) are protected.

faq.q: How much is a dog bite case worth?
faq.a: Depends on the severity of injuries, scarring, emotional trauma, and medical care needed. Moderate-severity dog bite cases often settle in the $20,000 to $100,000 range; severe cases involving facial scarring, child victims, or surgical reconstruction can settle in the high six or seven figures.

faq.q: How long do I have to file a dog bite lawsuit in California?
faq.a: Generally two years from the date of the bite under California's personal injury statute of limitations. For child victims, the deadline can be extended (the limitations period typically doesn't run while the victim is a minor).

faq.q: Can I recover for emotional trauma and scarring after a dog attack?
faq.a: Yes. California allows recovery for emotional distress, fear of dogs (cynophobia), pain and suffering, scarring/disfigurement, and future medical care including reconstructive surgery and counseling. Child victims often have particularly strong claims for emotional trauma.

faq.q: What if a child was bitten by a dog?
faq.a: Children's dog bite cases are often the most serious — bites to the face, multiple bite wounds, and lasting psychological trauma are common. California protects children's claims with extended limitations periods and court approval of any settlement to ensure the funds are properly preserved.

faq.q: What evidence is important in a dog bite case?
faq.a: Photos of injuries (taken immediately and during healing), the police or animal control report, the dog's vaccination records, witness statements, the owner's homeowners or renters insurance information, and your medical records. Animal control should always be notified — they document the dog's history.

faq.q: How much does it cost to hire a dog bite lawyer?
faq.a: Frank Penney Injury Law works on a contingency basis. You pay nothing up front and no fee unless we win. Free consultations are available 24/7 by phone at (888) 888-0566 or in person at any of our 6 California offices. We also make home and hospital visits.
